content.text: Python's type system is simpler and more uniform than most compiled languages: every value is an object, variables are references to objects, and the same name can refer to objects of completely different types over the course of a program's execution.
A variable in Python is not a typed container — it is a name bound to an object. When you write
, Python creates an integer object with value 42 and attaches the label
to it. When you later write
, the label is detached from the integer and reattached to a new string object. The integer itself may be garbage-collected if no other names refer to it.

Python's type system is often described as dynamic and strongly typed. Dynamic because variable names have no declared type — the type lives in the object, not the name. Strong because Python does not perform implicit coercions between unrelated types: adding a string and an integer raises a TypeError instead of silently converting one operand.
The eight fundamental built-in types that every Python program uses are: integers (
), and the major collections: lists, tuples, dicts, and sets. Each has a distinct purpose, distinct memory layout, and distinct performance characteristics.

For developers coming from statically-typed languages such as Java or C++, Python's approach can feel dangerously permissive. In practice, dynamic typing speeds up exploration and prototyping because you spend no time satisfying a type checker before you know what shape your data will take. For agentic AI workflows this matters enormously: LLM tool outputs, API responses, and user inputs all arrive as loosely structured data that must be inspected and routed at runtime.

Understanding the type of every value — especially the difference between mutable and immutable types, and between reference semantics and value semantics — prevents an entire class of subtle bugs. A list passed into a function and mutated inside that function affects the caller; an integer passed in and incremented inside the function does not. Knowing this distinction before writing your first agent that shares state between tools will save hours of debugging.
Python's type system also determines what operations are legal. Attempting to call

on a tuple, or index a dictionary with an unhashable key, or multiply a string by a float, all raise runtime errors. Understanding types helps you anticipate and prevent these errors long before they reach production.
No type declarations required, so early-stage code can be written quickly and refactored as the design clarifies.
The same core data structures — lists, dicts, sets — are used throughout the standard library and third-party ecosystem, so there is one mental model to learn and one API to remember.
Every object carries its type at runtime (
), so you can always inspect what you have when debugging.
Python's immutable types (int, str, tuple) are hashable by default and safe to use as dict keys or set members without defensive copying.
) can be added incrementally for tooling support without changing runtime behaviour.
Arithmetic between numeric types is automatically promoted upward: int + float produces a float, keeping precision without manual casting.
Runtime type errors that a static compiler would catch at build time only surface when the relevant code path is executed, which may be deep in a production workflow.
Large programs without type annotations are harder to reason about because function signatures do not communicate expected types — callers must read docstrings or source.
Dynamic typing makes some optimizations impossible for the Python interpreter, which is why Python programs are generally slower than equivalent C or Go programs for CPU-bound tasks.
Mutable default arguments in function signatures are a notorious trap: the default object is created once, not per call, leading to shared state across invocations.
Type coercion between numeric types (int ↔ float ↔ bool) can silently produce incorrect results in comparisons:
Python assignment works as name binding, not value copying. When you write
point to the same list object. Mutating through either name affects the same underlying object. The built-in
function returns the memory address of the object a name is bound to, making it easy to test whether two names refer to the same object.
operator tests object identity (same memory address), while
tests value equality. These often coincide for small integers (Python caches integer objects in the range -5 to 256) but diverge for larger integers, strings created at runtime, and lists.

Python integers are arbitrary precision: there is no overflow. An integer can hold 2^1000 or more, limited only by available memory. This is implemented by Python's bignum library. The consequence is that integer arithmetic is always exact, but very large integers require proportionally more memory and CPU time to operate on than machine-word integers in C.
Python floats are 64-bit IEEE 754 doubles, identical to the
type in C. They have 53 bits of mantissa, which gives roughly 15-16 significant decimal digits. The classic consequence:
in Python because 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be represented exactly in binary floating point. For financial calculations requiring exact decimal arithmetic, use the

Python strings are Unicode sequences of code points (Python 3 uses UTF-8 internally for storage). They are immutable: no method on a string modifies the string in place — all methods return new string objects. This makes strings safe to share across threads and safe to use as dictionary keys. String concatenation in a tight loop (
creates a new string object, making the loop O(n^2) in total work.
Python evaluates any object as a boolean in a conditional context by calling
on it. Objects that evaluate to False include:
). Everything else is truthy. This allows concise guards like
The diagram below shows Python's built-in type taxonomy grouped by category, along with the mutability distinction that determines whether objects can be modified after creation.

content.text: : Use for counts, indices, loop counters, or any quantity that cannot be fractional. Prefer int over float when exact arithmetic matters.
: Use for measurements, probabilities, loss values, and any quantity that can be fractional. Accept the ≈15-digit precision limit.
: Use for all text: names, paths, prompts, LLM outputs. Never use bytes for text unless working at the protocol level (HTTP, file I/O).
: Use for flags and conditions. Avoid storing booleans as integers or strings.
: Use as the explicit 'no value' sentinel. Function parameters that are optional and absent should default to None, not 0 or ''.
: Use when order matters and the collection will be modified after creation.
: Use for fixed-length ordered records — function return values, coordinate pairs, CSV rows. Tuples communicate 'this will not change'.
: Use for key-value associations where fast lookup by key is needed. The key must be hashable (immutable type).
: Use for membership testing and duplicate elimination. Much faster than list for 'is X in collection?' queries.
The following code block covers assignment, type inspection, mutability demonstration, and common patterns for each built-in type. Run it directly in any Python 3.8+ interpreter.

# Variables, types, and binding
x = 42
print(type(x))           # <class 'int'>
print(isinstance(x, int))  # True

# Rebinding: x changes type
x = 3.14
print(type(x))           # <class 'float'>

# Multiple assignment and unpacking
a, b, c = 1, 2, 3
first, *rest = [10, 20, 30, 40]
print(first, rest)       # 10 [20, 30, 40]

# Integer arithmetic — arbitrary precision
big = 2 ** 200
print(big)               # 1606938044258990275541962092341162602522202993782792835301376

# Float: 15-16 significant digits
print(0.1 + 0.2)         # 0.30000000000000004  (float representation)
print(round(0.1 + 0.2, 2) == 0.3)  # True — round for comparison

# String: immutable, unicode
s = "hello"
print(s.upper())         # HELLO (returns new string; s is unchanged)
print(s)                 # hello

# Bool: subclass of int
print(True + True)       # 2
print(isinstance(True, int))  # True

# None as sentinel
def get_value(key, mapping):
    return mapping.get(key)  # returns None if key absent

# id() to inspect identity
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a           # same object
c = a.copy()    # different object, same values
print(id(a) == id(b))  # True
print(id(a) == id(c))  # False
print(a == c)           # True (equal values, different objects)

# Type inspection
values = [42, 3.14, "text", True, None, [1, 2], (3, 4), {"k": "v"}, {1, 2}]
for v in values:
    print(f"{repr(v):<20} type={type(v).__name__:<12} id={id(v)}")

# Explicit type conversion (casting)
print(int("42"))          # 42
print(float("3.14"))      # 3.14
print(str(100))           # '100'
print(bool(0))            # False
print(bool(""))           # False
print(bool(" "))          # True  (non-empty string)
print(list("abc"))        # ['a', 'b', 'c']
print(tuple([1, 2, 3]))   # (1, 2, 3)
print(set([1, 2, 2, 3]))  # {1, 2, 3}

# Numeric type promotion
result = 5 + 2.0          # int + float -> float
print(type(result))       # <class 'float'>
print(result)             # 7.0

# Gotcha: integer division vs. true division
print(7 // 2)             # 3  (floor division, always int)
print(7 / 2)              # 3.5 (true division, always float)
print(7 % 2)              # 1   (modulo)

# Mutable types — aliasing danger
list_a = [1, 2, 3]
list_b = list_a          # alias — same object
list_b.append(4)
print(list_a)            # [1, 2, 3, 4]  — list_a was modified!

# Fix: make an explicit copy
list_a = [1, 2, 3]
list_c = list_a.copy()   # shallow copy — new list, same elements
list_c.append(4)
print(list_a)            # [1, 2, 3]  — unaffected
print(list_c)            # [1, 2, 3, 4]

# Immutable types — no aliasing problem
str_a = "hello"
str_b = str_a
str_b = str_b + " world"  # creates new string
print(str_a)             # "hello"  — unchanged

# Tuple vs list: identical data, different mutability
point_list = [3.0, 4.0]   # can be mutated
point_tuple = (3.0, 4.0)  # cannot be mutated
try:
    point_tuple[0] = 99   # raises TypeError
except TypeError as e:
    print(e)  # 'tuple' object does not support item assignment

# Hashing: immutable types are hashable, mutable types are not
print(hash("hello"))     # some integer
print(hash((1, 2, 3)))   # some integer
try:
    hash([1, 2, 3])      # TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
except TypeError as e:
    print(e)
Example 3: None, bool, and Falsy Values

# None as a sentinel
result = None

def fetch_data(key):
    store = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
    return store.get(key)  # returns None if not found

value = fetch_data("c")
if value is None:          # identity check — correct way to test for None
    print("Not found")

# bool is a subclass of int — use carefully
flags = [True, False, True, True]
print(sum(flags))          # 3 — counts True values
print(all(flags))          # False — not all are True
print(any(flags))          # True — at least one is True

# Falsy value pitfall in conditionals
count = 0
if count:
    print("Has items")    # NOT printed — 0 is falsy
else:
    print("Empty or zero")  # printed

# Better: be explicit when 0 is a valid value to distinguish from None
count = 0
if count is not None:
    print("Count set:", count)  # printed — 0 is a valid count

In an agentic AI pipeline, tools frequently return structured data from API calls. The agent must inspect, validate, and route that data before acting on it. The following shows a realistic pattern where type checking, None handling, and type conversion all appear together.

# WRONG: the list is created once and shared across all calls
def append_item(item, accumulator=[]):
    accumulator.append(item)
    return accumulator

print(append_item("a"))   # ['a']
print(append_item("b"))   # ['a', 'b']  — unexpected! same list reused
print(append_item("c"))   # ['a', 'b', 'c']

# CORRECT: use None as the default, create fresh list inside
def append_item_fixed(item, accumulator=None):
    if accumulator is None:
        accumulator = []
    accumulator.append(item)
    return accumulator

print(append_item_fixed("a"))  # ['a']
print(append_item_fixed("b"))  # ['b']  — fresh list each time
Mistake 2: Using == Instead of is for None

# WRONG: == can be overridden by __eq__; for None, always use is
value = None
if value == None:    # works, but not idiomatic; fragile if value overrides __eq__
    pass

# CORRECT
if value is None:    # identity check — always correct for None and True/False
    pass
if value is not None:
    pass

# WRONG: expecting integer result from /
result = 7 / 2
print(result)        # 3.5, not 3 — / always returns float in Python 3
print(type(result))  # <class 'float'>

# CORRECT: use // for floor division when you need an int
result = 7 // 2
print(result)        # 3
print(type(result))  # <class 'int'>

# Also: divmod gives both quotient and remainder
q, r = divmod(7, 2)
print(q, r)          # 3 1
Use type annotations on function signatures (
) to document intent, enable editor autocomplete, and support static analysis tools like mypy and pyright.
for type checks — isinstance handles inheritance correctly (e.g., bool is a subclass of int).
as the explicit absent-value sentinel. Avoid using 0, -1, or empty string as sentinels — they may be valid data values.
Prefer tuples over lists for fixed-size records. The immutability communicates intent and makes the data hashable.
For equality comparisons involving floats, never use
or round to a fixed number of decimals.
over repeated string concatenation in loops. The join is O(n) total; repeated += is O(n^2).
comparisons for duck typing. If you only need a specific method to exist, check with
Python integers have no overflow but allocate heap memory for each object. Small integers (-5 to 256) are cached as singletons. This means
may be False. This caching is an implementation detail; never rely on it in code.
String interning (Python reusing the same object for identical string literals that look like identifiers) is similarly an implementation detail. Do not use
to compare strings in general — always use

For large numerical datasets, the built-in numeric types are insufficient for performance. NumPy arrays are contiguous blocks of C-level typed values (int32, float64, etc.) and perform arithmetic at compiled-C speed. A NumPy operation on a million floats takes microseconds; an equivalent Python loop takes hundreds of milliseconds.
* Tuples are hashable only if all their elements are hashable. A tuple containing a list is not hashable.
: The reference static type checker for Python. Run
to catch type errors without executing code.
: Microsoft's type checker, used by Pylance in VS Code. Faster than mypy and more strict by default.
: Runtime type validation using Python type annotations — widely used in FastAPI and LLM pipelines to validate structured data.

# Built-in tools for type inspection
x = [1, "hello", 3.14]
print(type(x))           # <class 'list'>
print(dir(x))            # all methods and attributes of x
help(x.append)           # documentation for the append method

# Python 3.10+ structural pattern matching
value = {"action": "search", "query": "python types"}
match value:
    case {"action": "search", "query": str(q)}:
        print(f"Searching for: {q}")
    case {"action": "store", "data": list(items)}:
        print(f"Storing {len(items)} items")
    case _:
        print("Unknown action")
Python and Ruby share the dynamic-typing-with-bignum-integers philosophy. JavaScript is superficially similar but much weaker: it silently converts types in arithmetic (
), whereas Python raises TypeError. Java's explicit type declarations prevent the mutable-default-argument class of bugs at compile time but require more ceremony for exploratory code.
Accepting user input and passing it directly to
is dangerous regardless of type. When parsing user-provided data that might be a dict, list, or primitive, use
on validated JSON strings rather than
on untrusted input, and never use

Python itself never overflows integers, but when calling C extensions, NumPy, or external libraries that accept fixed-width integers, passing a Python int that is too large raises an OverflowError or silently wraps. Always range-check integers before passing them to C-level APIs or serializing them to formats like uint32.
in production code (e.g., checking whether a probability threshold is exactly 0.5) produces wrong results when floating-point rounding differs between platforms or Python versions. Use
with an appropriate tolerance, or avoid float comparisons in decision logic by scaling to integers.

import math

# WRONG: fragile float comparison
score = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.2
if score == 0.5:             # False — floating-point sum is 0.5000000000000001
    print("threshold met")

# CORRECT: tolerance-based comparison
if math.isclose(score, 0.5, rel_tol=1e-9):
    print("threshold met")  # This prints

# Or: multiply by 1000 and compare integers for fixed-precision thresholds
score_int = round(score * 1000)
if score_int == 500:
    print("threshold met")  # This prints

Think of Python variables as sticky notes placed on objects. The sticky note (the name) is not the object — it is just a label. You can peel the sticky note off one object and slap it on a completely different object with no ceremony. Multiple sticky notes can be placed on the same object; removing one note does not destroy the object as long as other notes remain.

Mutability maps to a simple physical intuition: immutable objects are like stone carvings — the text is there permanently, and if you want different text you must carve a new stone. Mutable objects are like whiteboards — you can erase and rewrite without creating a new board. This means two people (names) pointing at the same whiteboard will both see any changes; two people pointing at the same stone carving might not care since they can never change it.
The type lives in the object, not the name. When you call
, Python is asking the object itself 'what are you?' — the name
has no type information of its own.
Python variables are names bound to objects, not typed storage slots. The type is a property of the object, not the variable name.
Python is dynamically typed (types checked at runtime) and strongly typed (no implicit coercion between unrelated types). Both properties matter for writing correct code.

The mutable/immutable distinction determines whether multiple names sharing the same object can observe each other's mutations. Lists, dicts, and sets are mutable; int, float, str, tuple, and bool are immutable.
Immutable types are hashable and can be used as dict keys or set elements. Mutable types cannot.
Floats are IEEE 754 doubles with ~15 decimal digits of precision. Never compare floats with ==; use math.isclose() or integer rounding.
Python integers are arbitrary-precision and never overflow. This is unlike virtually every other language and is one of Python's most convenient properties for algorithmic work.
Avoid mutable default arguments in function definitions. Use None as the default and create the mutable object inside the function body.
For high-performance numerical work, prefer NumPy arrays over Python lists of floats. NumPy provides typed, contiguous, SIMD-friendly storage that is orders of magnitude faster for bulk operations.
